audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Abram's call, blessing/name/land promise, movement toward Canaan, altar-building, famine, Sarai sister-wife episode, and Pharaoh plague. Final source-critical review may refine geography and idiom.

verses.restored: And the Eternal said to Abram, Go for yourself from your land, from your kindred, and from your father’s house, to the land that I will show you.

familiar.text: Now the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred...

notes.title: Go for yourself
notes.body: Lekh lekha is an emphatic call to go forth. It can carry the sense of go for yourself, go to yourself, or simply go.

notes.title: Threefold leaving
notes.body: Land, kindred, and father’s house describe a total rupture from inherited identity and security.

verses.restored: I will make you into a great nation, and I will bless you, and I will make your name great. And you shall be a blessing.

familiar.text: And I will make of thee a great nation, and I will bless thee, and make thy name great; and thou shalt be a blessing:

notes.title: Name made by promise
notes.body: After Babel’s self-made name, Abram’s name is made by divine blessing.

notes.title: Be a blessing
notes.body: The promise is not only possession but vocation: Abram becomes a channel of blessing.

verses.restored: I will bless those who bless you, and the one who treats you lightly I will curse. And in you all the families of the ground shall be blessed.

familiar.text: And I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee: and in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed.

notes.title: Families of the ground
notes.body: Mishpachot ha-adamah recalls the ground/humanity language. The blessing aims outward to all families, not only Abram’s private line.

notes.title: Treats you lightly
notes.body: The first verb for curse here can carry the sense of treating lightly or dishonoring; the answering divine curse uses a stronger term.

verses.restored: So Abram went, as the Eternal had spoken to him, and Lot went with him. Abram was seventy-five years old when he went out from Haran.

familiar.text: So Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken unto him; and Lot went with him...

notes.title: Went as spoken
notes.body: Abram's first response is movement aligned with the divine word.

verses.restored: Abram took Sarai his woman, Lot his brother’s son, all their possessions that they had gathered, and the persons they had acquired in Haran; and they went out to go to the land of Canaan. They came to the land of Canaan.

familiar.text: And Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son, and all their substance...

notes.title: Persons acquired
notes.body: The phrase includes members of the household, servants, or dependents acquired in Haran. The social world is patriarchal household-based.

verses.restored: Abram passed through the land to the place of Shechem, to the oak of Moreh. The Canaanite was then in the land.

familiar.text: And Abram passed through the land unto the place of Sichem, unto the plain of Moreh...

notes.title: Oak of Moreh
notes.body: Elon Moreh may mean oak/terebinth of Moreh. Sacred trees often mark places of encounter.

notes.title: Canaanite in the land
notes.body: The promise is made in occupied land, creating tension that will shape the narrative.

verses.restored: The Eternal appeared to Abram and said, To your seed I will give this land. And there he built an altar to the Eternal, who had appeared to him.

familiar.text: And the LORD appeared unto Abram, and said, Unto thy seed will I give this land...

notes.title: Seed and land
notes.body: The promise joins offspring and land. Abram responds by building an altar.

verses.restored: From there he moved to the hill country east of Bethel and pitched his tent, with Bethel on the west and Ai on the east. There he built an altar to the Eternal and called on the name of the Eternal.

familiar.text: And he removed from thence unto a mountain on the east of Bethel... and called upon the name of the LORD.

notes.title: Called on the name
notes.body: Abram continues the earlier practice of invoking the name of the Eternal.

verses.restored: Then Abram journeyed on, going and journeying toward the Negev.

familiar.text: And Abram journeyed, going on still toward the south.

notes.title: Negev
notes.body: Negev is the southern dry region; south is interpretive but Negev keeps the place-name.

verses.restored: There was a famine in the land, and Abram went down to Egypt to sojourn there, for the famine was heavy in the land.

familiar.text: And there was a famine in the land: and Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there...

notes.title: Went down to Egypt
notes.body: Egypt becomes a place of survival under famine, a pattern that will recur in Genesis.

verses.restored: When he drew near to enter Egypt, he said to Sarai his woman, Look, I know that you are a woman beautiful in appearance.

familiar.text: And it came to pass, when he was come near to enter into Egypt, that he said unto Sarai his wife...

notes.title: Beautiful in appearance
notes.body: Abram’s fear arises around Sarai’s beauty and the danger he expects from Egyptian power.

verses.restored: When the Egyptians see you, they will say, This is his woman; then they will kill me, but you they will keep alive.

familiar.text: Therefore it shall come to pass, when the Egyptians shall see thee... they will kill me, but they will save thee alive.

notes.title: Fear-driven strategy
notes.body: Abram imagines danger to himself and survival for Sarai, setting up the sister-wife deception.

verses.restored: Please say you are my sister, so that it may go well with me because of you, and my life may be kept alive on account of you.

familiar.text: Say, I pray thee, thou art my sister: that it may be well with me for thy sake...

notes.title: Sister-wife deception
notes.body: Abram asks Sarai to protect his life through a partial or strategic kinship claim. The text does not praise the action.

verses.restored: When Abram came into Egypt, the Egyptians saw that the woman was very beautiful.

familiar.text: And it came to pass, that, when Abram was come into Egypt, the Egyptians beheld the woman that she was very fair.

verses.restored: The officials of Pharaoh saw her and praised her to Pharaoh, and the woman was taken into Pharaoh’s house.

familiar.text: The princes also of Pharaoh saw her, and commended her before Pharaoh: and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house.

notes.title: Taken
notes.body: Laqach, took, is a key power word. Sarai is taken into Pharaoh’s house, exposing her vulnerability in the scheme.

verses.restored: He treated Abram well because of her, and he had sheep, cattle, male donkeys, male servants, female servants, female donkeys, and camels.

familiar.text: And he entreated Abram well for her sake: and he had sheep, and oxen...

notes.title: Benefit through Sarai
notes.body: Abram gains wealth because of Sarai’s being taken, a morally difficult element the translation should not soften.

verses.restored: But the Eternal struck Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai, Abram’s woman.

familiar.text: And the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai Abram's wife.

notes.title: Divine intervention for Sarai
notes.body: The Eternal intervenes when Sarai is in Pharaoh’s house. The plagues anticipate later Exodus patterns.

verses.restored: Pharaoh called Abram and said, What is this you have done to me? Why did you not tell me that she was your woman?

familiar.text: And Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me? why didst thou not tell me...

notes.title: What have you done?
notes.body: Pharaoh’s question echoes accountability language from earlier Genesis scenes.

verses.restored: Why did you say, She is my sister, so that I took her for myself as a woman? Now then, here is your woman. Take her and go.

familiar.text: Why saidst thou, She is my sister? so I might have taken her to me to wife...

notes.title: Take her and go
notes.body: Pharaoh returns Sarai and expels Abram, reversing the earlier taking.

verses.restored: Pharaoh commanded men concerning him, and they sent him away with his woman and all that belonged to him.

familiar.text: And Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him: and they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had.

notes.title: Sent away
notes.body: Abram leaves Egypt with Sarai and wealth, again anticipating later Exodus motifs.
